content: If $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ are two non-zero complex numbers, then which of the following is not true?
The real value of ' $\alpha$ ' for which $\frac{1-i \sin \alpha}{1+2 i \sin \alpha}$ is purely real is
explanation:
Given, $\frac{1-i \sin \alpha}{1+2 i \sin \alpha}$ is purely real

i.e. $\frac{1-i \sin \alpha}{1+2 i \sin \alpha} \times \frac{(1-2 i \sin \alpha)}{(1-2 i \sin \alpha)}$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1-i \sin \alpha-2 i \sin \alpha+2 i^2 \sin ^2 \alpha}{1-4 i^2 \sin ^2 \alpha} \\ & =\frac{1-3 i \sin \alpha-2 \sin ^2 \alpha}{1+4 \sin ^2 \alpha} \\ & =\frac{1-2 \sin ^2 \alpha}{1+4 \sin ^2 \alpha}+i\left(\frac{-3 \sin \alpha}{1+4 \sin ^2 \alpha}\right) \end{aligned}$$

which is given to purely real

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \frac{-3 \sin \alpha}{1+4 \sin ^2 \alpha}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & -3 \sin \alpha=0 \Rightarrow \sin \alpha=0 \end{array}$$

Hence, $\alpha=n \pi, n \in N$

content: The modulus of the complex number $$\frac{(1+i)^2(1+3 i)}{(2-6 i)(2-2 i)}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text { Here, } \quad & \frac{(1+i)^2(1+3 i)}{(2-6 i)(2-2 i)} \\ = & \frac{\left(1+i^2+2 i\right)(1+3 i)}{4-4 i-12 i+12 i^2} \\ = & \frac{2 i+6 i^2}{-8-16 i}=\frac{2 i-6}{-8-16 i} \\ = & \frac{i-3}{-4-8 i} \\ = & \frac{3-i}{(4+8 i)} \times \frac{(4-8 i)}{(4-8 i)} \\ = & \frac{12-28 i-8}{16+64} \\ = & \frac{4-28 i}{80}=\frac{1-7 i}{20} \\ = & \frac{1}{20}-\frac{7 i}{20} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } z=x+i y \\ & \because|z|=\sqrt{x^2+y^2} \\ & \Rightarrow|z|=\sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{20}\right)^2+\left(\frac{7}{20}\right)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow|z|=\frac{1}{20} \sqrt{50}=\frac{5}{20} \sqrt{2} \\ & \Rightarrow|z|=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{4} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$3 x+i(4 x-y)=6-i$$ where $$x$$ and $$y$$ are real numbers, then the values of $$x$$ and $$y$$ are respectively,
explanation:
$$3 x+i(4 x-y)=6-i$$

On comparing real and imaginary parts of LHS and RHS, we get

$$\begin{aligned} 3 x & =6 \text { and } 4 x-y=-1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 3 x & =6 \Rightarrow x=2 \end{aligned}$$

Put $$x=2$$ into $$4 x-y=-1$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 4 \times 2-y=-1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 8+1=y \Rightarrow y=9 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text {Given, }\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)^x=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left[\frac{(1+i)}{1-i)} \times \frac{(1+i)}{(1+i)}\right]^x=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left[\frac{1+i^2+2 i}{1^2-i^2}\right]^x=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left[\frac{1-1+2 i}{1+1}\right]^x=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad i^x=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad i^x=1=i^{4 n} \text { where } n \in N \\ & \therefore x=4 n, n \in N. \\ \end{aligned}$$

To solve the given expression $\left(\frac{1-i}{1+i}\right)^{96} = a + ib$, follow these steps:

Simplify the Fraction:

$ \frac{1-i}{1+i} $

Multiply the numerator and the denominator by the conjugate of the denominator:

$ \frac{(1-i)(1-i)}{(1+i)(1-i)} $

Calculate the Numerator and Denominator:

$ (1-i)(1-i) = 1 - 2i + i^2 = 1 - 2i - 1 = -2i $

$ (1+i)(1-i) = 1 - i^2 = 1 - (-1) = 2 $

Simplified Expression:

$ \frac{-2i}{2} = -i $

Raise to the Power of 96:

$ (-i)^{96} $

Understanding Powers of $-i$:

Since $(-i)^4 = 1$, calculate $(-$i$))^{96}$:

$ (-i)^{96} = ((-i)^4)^{24} = 1^{24} = 1 $

Thus, the expression simplifies to:

$ a + ib = 1 + 0i $

    Result:

    Therefore, $(a, b) = (1, 0)$.

content: If $\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)^m=1$, then the least positive integral value of $m$ is
